rulings.Case Title: MINT COLLECTION -410-418 JESSIE STREET CONDOMINIUM VS. FIFTH HISTORIC PROPERTIES, LLC, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANTs STATION HOUSE C1, LLC,, VC FUND C-1, LLC, and VC MULTIFAMILY PORTFOLIO VENTURE GP, LLC DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants Station House C1, LLC, VC Multifamily Portfolio Venture GP, LLC and VC Fund C-1, LLC's demurrer to the first through fourth and sixth through tenth causes of action in the second amended complaint is: a) sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for violation of residential building standards, the third cause of action for strict liability, the sixth cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty, the seventh cause of action for violation of governing documents, the eighth cause of action for unlawful LLC distributions, the ninth cause of action for breach of contract, and the tenth cause of action for negligence; and b) sustained with thirty days leave to amend as to the second cause of action for negligence and the fourth cause of action for breach of implied warranty of merchantability. The court has previously sustained demurrers to the first, third, and eighth causes of action without leave to amend as to defendants who are identically situated to the demurring defendants as to these claims and thus the demurrer to those claims is sustained without leave to amend. In their opposition memorandum plaintiff agreed to dismiss the sixth, seventh, ninth and tenth causes of action as to the demurring defendants and thus the demurrer to those claims is sustained without leave to amend. The second and fourth causes of action do not allege sufficient ultimate facts to state viable claims against the demurring defendants. If they can do so in good faith, plaintiff is given leave to allege ultimate facts supporting the second and fourth causes of action against the demurring defendants. As stated in a previous demurrer order, plaintiff should allege why it contends each of these defendants is liable instead of lumping them all together in a collectively defined term. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the demurring defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: UYEN NGUYEN VS. ROBERT HALF INTERNATIONAL INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT ROBERT HALF INTERNATIONAL INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication Defendant Robert Half International Inc.'s motion for summary judgment and its alternative motion for summary adjudication are denied in their entirety. As to plaintiff Uyen Nguyen's first cause of action for FEHA gender discrimination, Ms. Nguyen satisfied her light burden of presenting evidence showing that she can establish a prima facie case. That evidence includes that Ms. Nguyen was performing competently after returning to work from her third maternity leave and she was treated differently from similarly situated employees who were not written up or terminated allowing for an inference of discriminatory motive. There is a triable dispute whether Ms. Nguyen's pregnancy, coupled with her communicated intention to seek a leave to care for her mother only five months after she had returned from her third maternity leave, was substantial factor in RHI's decision to terminate her based on evidence that: Ms. Nguyen was not credited for all of her work after return from maternity leave; nevertheless Ms. Nguyen was written up for poor performance soon after she told her manager, Ms. Caldwell, that her mother was diagnosed with cancer; Ms. Nguyen was terminated one day after she reminded Ms. Caldwell that she planned on taking a leave to take care of her mother; Ms. Nguyen ranked fifth out of twelve in performance among similarly situated employees based on statistical analysis and these other employees were not written up and terminated. As to the second cause of action for FEHA associational discrimination, although Ms. Nguyen did not tell Ms. Johnston about her mother's cancer diagnosis and her intention of taking a leave, Ms. Nguyen testified that she did tell Ms. Caldwell, who was involved in the termination decision. Per the "cat's paw" doctrine, Ms. Nguyen's failure to inform Ms. Johnston does not preclude liability against RHI. (Reeves v. Safeway Stores, Inc. (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 95, 113). Although temporal proximity is not enough by itself to create a triable issue of pretext, Ms. Nguyen also proffered evidence that RHI falsely asserted she was under performing and treated her differently than other similarly situated employees. There is a triable dispute whether the termination of Ms. Nguyen's employment was impermissibly motivated by Ms. Nguyen's taking a maternity leave and her reasonable notice for exercise of her right to a leave under CFRA precluding summary adjudication in favor of RHI of Ms. Nguyen's third, fifth and sixth causes of action for FEHA retaliation and CFRA interference and retaliation. For the same reasons HRI is not entitled to a summary adjudication of the fourth, and seventh through ninth causes of action for FEHA failure to prevent discrimination, common law wrongful termination in violation of public policy, and declaratory and injunctive relief. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Nguyen is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: LAURIE BAKER-FLYNN VS. WESTERN GENERAL INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Western General Insurance Company'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 5, part 2 of 2 tentative rulings Defendant Western General Insurance Company'S Motion For Summary Judgment Nor did Western General satisfy its summary motion burden of showing that Ms. Baker-Flynn's first cause of action for recovery on the judgment per Insurance Code 11580(b)(2) is barred by the one year "statute of limitations" in the policy issued to Mr. Al Maliki. Assuming without deciding that the one year limitations period is an enforceable as to Ms. Baker-Flynn, her section 11580 claim did not accrue until judgment was entered in her favor and against Mr. Al Maliki on June 20, 2017, less than one month before this case was filed July 11, 2017. rulings.Case Title: LAURIE BAKER-FLYNN VS. WESTERN GENERAL INSURANCE COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Western General Insurance Companys Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 5, part 1 of 2 tentative rulings Defendant Western General Insurance Company's Motion For Summary Judgment Defendant Western General Insurance Company' motion for summary judgment is denied. Since Western General only moved for summary judgment and did not alternatively move for summary adjudication as to either or both of the two causes of action alleged by plaintiff Laurie Baker-Flynn, the motion can only be granted if Western General is entitled to judgment on both of Ms. Baker-Flynn's claims. Western General failed to satisfy its summary motion burden showing that it is entitled to judgment on the grounds that it validly rescinded policy WF000019362 issued to Muaad Al Maliki. Treating Ms. Baker-Flynn's arguments regarding the asserted insufficiency of the declaration of Marc O'Connell as an objection to paragraphs 12 and 13 of that declaration based on lack of foundation and hearsay, the court sustains those objections. Mr. O'Connell's statements regarding the materiality of Mr. Al Maliki's alleged misrepresentations are conclusory and not based on any facts showing that Mr. O'Connell has knowledge of Western General's policies and practices concerning the review, acceptance or rejection of insurance applications where the applicant answered yes to the commercial vehicle and public conveyance questions and/or stated that he drove for a ride-sharing company such as Lyft. Mr. O'Connell states that he is an attorney and does not provide any facts showing that he is involved in or knowledgeable about Western General's underwriting of policies. Mr. O'Connell states that his declaration is based on his "background and experience with Western General," his "review of the claims file specific to the Policy," and his "consultation with Western General employees." Yet Mr. O'Connell provides no facts regarding any relevant background and experience that he may have and does not state who he consulted with or what those people told him and, in all events, what he was told by others is inadmissible hearsay. Once the objection to paragraphs 12 and 13 of Mr. O'Connell's declaration is sustained, Western General has no evidence showing that the alleged misrepresentations made by Mr. Al Maliki were material and thus it cannot satisfy its burden of showing that the policy issued to Mr. Al Maliki was validly rescinded due to material misstatements by Mr. Al Maliki.

rulings.Case Title: LESLIE LAPAYOWKER VS. AIRBNB,INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Plaintiffs Motion To Reconsider The Prior Order To Set Aside Entry Of Default
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 6, PLAINTIFF LESLIE LAPAYOWKER's Motion To Reconsider The Prior Order To Set Aside Entry Of Default Plaintiff Leslie Lapayowker's motion to reconsider the March 27, 2018 order granting defendant Carlos Del Olmo's motion to set aside the default entered against him on February 13, 2018 is treated as an invitation to the court to reconsider the March 27, 2018 order per Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal. 4th 1094 and, as so treated, is granted and the March 27, 2018 order is vacated and the February 13, 2018 default is reinstated. Ms. Lapayowker has shown that the March 27, 2018 order was issued based on false declarations from Mr. Del Olmo and Daniel Pires. In particular, the screenshot of the December 10, 2017 5:25pm text message attached as exhibit B to Mr. Pires' most recent declaration, when read together with all other evidence on this motion and the motion to set aside the default, is persuasive evidence not squarely refuted by Mr. Del Olmo that service of the summons and complaint was made on Mr. Del Olmo on December 10, 2017. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Lapayowker is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: DAVID DOBSON VS. 3525 17TH STREET PARTNERS, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff David Dobson'S Motion To Strike Defendants 3525 17th Street Partners, Llc And Steven Mavromihalis Memorandum Of Costs; In The Alternative, To Tax Costs. (Advanced From 10/5/18 Per Judge Kahn)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 12, Plaintiff David Dobson'S Motion To Strike Defendants 3525 17th Street Partners, Llc And Steven Mavromihalis Memorandum Of Costs; In The Alternative, To Tax Costs. (Advanced From 10/5/18 Per Judge Kahn) Plaintiffs' motion to strike defendants' memorandum of costs is granted. All costs claimed by defendants in the costs memorandum filed August 7, 2018 are stricken. Neither plaintiffs nor defendants prevailed on a "practical level" and thus, per the exercise of the court's discretion, neither side is the prevailing party for purposes of an award of costs per CCP 1032(a)(4). (Gilbert v. National Enquirer, Inc. (1997) 55 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1277-78). The parties' legal relations pertaining to the occupancy of the units formerly occupied by plaintiffs as a result of the 2016 fire have not been finally determined. After the filing of this lawsuit defendants apprised plaintiffs of pertinent information that had not been provided to them prior to the lawsuit. Plaintiffs' contention that they would not have received this information but for the filing of the lawsuit appears to be both plausible and comports with the allegation in paragraph 15 of the complaint that defendants "stopped providing updates to" the plaintiffs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, September 27, 2018, Line 12, DEFENDANTs 3525 17TH STREET PARTNERS, LLC., and STEVEN MAVROMIHALIS' MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES Defendants' motion for an award of statutory attorneys' fees is denied. Neither plaintiffs nor defendants prevailed on a "practical level" and thus, per the exercise of the court's discretion, neither side is the prevailing party for purposes of an award of fees per section 37.9(f) of the Rent Ordinance. (Gilbert v. National Enquirer, Inc. (1997) 55 Cal. App. 4th 1273, 1277-78). The parties' legal relations pertaining to the occupancy of the units formerly occupied by plaintiffs as a result of the 2016 fire have not been finally determined. After the filing of this lawsuit defendants apprised plaintiffs of pertinent information that had not been provided to them prior to the lawsuit. Plaintiffs' contention that they would not have received this information but for the filing of the lawsuit appears to be both plausible and comports with the allegation in paragraph 15 of the complaint that defendants "stopped providing updates to" the plaintiffs. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
